incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect equation on first attempt

steps.text: Check the bags. How many bags do you see? How many apples in each?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect equation on second attempt

params.description: Bags highlight to draw attention to the groups.

steps.text: Let's count together. I see 3 bags. In each bag, I count 4 apples. The first slot needs the number of bags. The second slot needs how many are in each bag. The last slot is the total.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect equation three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing tiles: first 3, then 4, then 12. Each tile slides into position.

steps.text: Here's how this works. First, I count the bags: 3. That goes in the first slot. Then I count what's in each bag: 4. That goes in the second slot. Finally, I count all the apples: 12. That's the product, so it goes in the last slot. The equation is 3 times 4 equals 12. 3 groups of 4 equals 12 total.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers on first attempt

steps.text: Check the picture. How many boxes? How many crayons in each box?

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers on second attempt

params.description: Boxes highlight one at a time: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

steps.text: Let's count together. Count the boxes first: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. That's your first number. Now count what's in one box. How many crayons?

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight one at a time as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
Items in first box highlight as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.

steps.text: Let me show you. First I count the boxes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. That's the first number, 6 groups. Then I count what's in one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. That's 7 in each group. So I place 6, then 7, then the product. 6 times 7. Skip count by 7s six times: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. The answer is 6 times 7 equals 42. Six boxes, 7 crayons in each, 42 total.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers on first attempt

steps.text: Look closely. Count the bags. Count what's inside each bag.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers on second attempt

params.description: Bags highlight one at a time: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Here's what helps. Count the bags first: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. That gives you the groups. Now look inside one bag and count the stars. What do you get?

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight one at a time as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
Stars in first bag highlight as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. I start by counting the bags: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. That's 5 groups. Then I pick one bag and count the stars inside: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. That's 9 in each group. So the equation is 5 times 9. Now I need the product. I can skip count by 9s five times: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. The answer is 5 times 9 equals 45. Five bags, 9 stars in each, 45 total. That's how the equation matches the picture.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt

steps.text: Check the picture. Count the boxes, count what's in each box.

params.description: The 3 boxes highlight.

steps.text: Three boxes. Count the cookies in one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8. That's 8 cookies per box. Now figure out the product — 3 groups of 8. Place those numbers into the equation.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight one at a time: 1, 2, 3.
Cookies in first box highlight one at a time: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.
Tiles place into equation one at a time: 24 in product slot, 3 in groups slot, 8 in items slot.

steps.text: Here's how this works. I count the boxes: 1, 2, 3. Three groups. I count the cookies in one box: 8 cookies. So I have 3 groups of 8. Three times 8 equals 24. That's the product. The equation is 24 equals 3 times 8. Product first, expression second. Same value on both sides.

steps.text: Not quite. Count the bags, then count what's in each bag.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, count the bags: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four bags, that's the first number. Now look inside one bag and count: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six marbles in each bag, that's the second number. Four groups of 6. Now multiply: 4 times 6 equals 24. The equation is 4 times 6 equals 24.

params.description: Equal groups visual appears. 3 horizontal rows of books on shelves. 9 books per row. Books arranged in clear horizontal rows.
Equation builder appears with blank multiplication equation template. Tile palette shows numbers 3, 4, 8, 9, 10, 12, 24, 27, 30, 36.

beats.text: Equal groups don't just come in bags and boxes. They're everywhere. Rows of books. Stacks of plates. Groups of animals. The math works the same way.

elements.description: Equal groups visual showing 3 rows of books on shelves. 9 books per row.
Equation builder with blank multiplication template. Palette available.

beats.text: Look at these shelves. I see rows of books. Each row is a group, just like each bag was a group.

params.description: Rows highlight one at a time in sequence.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3. Three rows. That's 3 groups. And in each row?

elements.description: 3 rows of books. All rows highlighted after sequential animation.
Equation builder with blank template.

params.description: Items in first row highlight one at a time as guide counts.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Nine books. So I've got 3 rows of 9. Same pattern: groups of items.

elements.description: 3 rows of books. First row's items highlighted.
Equation builder with blank template.

beats.text: 3 times 9. And the product? Skip count by 9s: 9, 18, 27.

params.description: Guide demonstration: tiles place into equation. 3 × 9 = 27 appears.

beats.text: 3 times 9 equals 27. Same equation building, different picture.

elements.description: 3 rows of books on shelves. First row highlighted.
Equation builder showing completed equation: 3 × 9 = 27.

params.description: Equation reverses to show commutative form: 27 = 3 × 9.

elements.description: 3 rows of books on shelves.
Equation builder showing reversed form: 27 = 3 × 9.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only A or only C (correct options only, but incomplete)

params.description: Both A and C highlight to show they are the equal groups examples.

steps.text: You found one of them. Look at both A and C closely. They both show equal groups. Did you select both?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect options (B and/or D), no correct answers

params.description: B and D highlight to draw attention to their unequal structure. A and C dim.

steps.text: Equal groups means the same number in every group. Look at B: 5, 6, 5, 2. Those aren't equal. Look at D: 7, 4, 7. Those aren't equal either. Find the pictures where every group has exactly the same count.

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected a mix of correct (A or C) and incorrect (B or D)

params.description: A and C pulse green (equal groups). B and D pulse red (unequal groups).

steps.text: You found at least one equal group picture. But check the others you selected. Count the items in each group. Are they all the same number?

incorrects.description: Heavy: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights A: arrows point to each shelf showing 6, 6, 6. Then highlights C: arrows point to each stack showing 9, 9. Then shows B: 5, 6, 5, 2 with X marks. Then D: 7, 4, 7 with X marks.

steps.text: Let me show you. Picture A has 3 shelves: 6 jars, 6 jars, 6 jars. Equal groups. Picture C has 2 stacks: 9 books, 9 books. Equal groups. Picture B has 4 plates: 5, 6, 5, 2. Not equal. Picture D has 3 groups: 7 ducks, 4 ducks, 7 ducks. Not equal. A and C are the answers because every group in those pictures has the same count. That is what equal groups means.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Skip count by 4s, once for each stack. 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. How many times did we count? Eight times. What's the total?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System counts plates sequentially: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. Running total appears under each stack.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. We skip count by 4s, once for each stack: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. Eight stacks of 4 plates equals 32 total. That's what 8 times 4 means. The product is 32.

steps.text: Let me show you. First shelf, 8 jars. Second shelf, 8 jars. 8 plus 8 equals 16. Or we can skip count by 8s: 8, 16. Either way, 2 times 8 equals 16. That's the product.

beats.text: Here's the new unknown. I know there are 5 birds in each group. And I know the total is 20 birds. But I don't know how many groups. That's the unknown.

beats.text: Let me read the sentence: HOW MANY groups of 5 equals 20 total? I need to figure out how many groups of 5 make 20.

incorrects.description: Student built 3 groups of 10, total = 30, short of target

steps.text: That's 3 groups of 10, which is 30. You need 40 total. Add one more group.

steps.text: That's 60, too many. Remove groups until you reach 40.

steps.text: Count by 10s as you add groups. Stop when you hit 40.

params.description: Running total display emphasized: Total: 0 / 40. Target of 40 highlighted.

steps.text: You need exactly 40. Count by 10s: one group is 10, two groups is 20, three groups is 30. How many groups to reach 40?

incorrects.description: Student built wrong number of groups three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Guide demonstrates building groups: one group (10), two groups (20), three groups (30), four groups (40). Each group appears sequentially with running count.

steps.text: Let me show you. One group of 10 is 10. Two groups is 20. Three groups is 30. Four groups is 40. It takes 4 groups of 10 to make 40. The unknown was 4.

incorrects.description: Student put 4 in each group, total = 8, short of target

steps.text: That's 8 total. You need 10. Add one more to each group.

steps.text: That's 12, too many. Remove one from each group.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number per group on second attempt

params.description: Running total display emphasized: Total shows current count / 10. Target of 10 highlighted.

steps.text: You have 2 groups. Each group needs the same number. Count by that number twice to reach 10. Try different amounts and watch the total.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number per group three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Guide demonstrates building: 5 cats appear in first group, then 5 in second group. Running count shows: 5, then 10.

steps.text: Let me show you. If I put 5 in the first group, that's 5. Then 5 in the second group brings the total to 10. Two groups of 5 equals 10. The unknown was 5.

beats.text: You built equations with bags, rows, stacks, and groups. You found unknown numbers in different positions. Let's see what you know.
